title: Understanding Damping Mechanisms via Spin Diffusion Length in Low-damping Li$_{0.5}$Al$_{1.0}$Fe$_{1.5}$O$_4$ Spinel Ferrite Thin Films
authors: Katya Mikhailova, Lerato Takana, Guanxiong Qu, Juan A. Hofer, Hervé M. Carruzzo, Ivan K. Schuller, Clare C. Yu, Yuri Suzuki
abstract: The mechanisms underlying magnon damping are of fundamental and technological interest in low-damping materials. We find low-damping ferrimagnetic insulator Li$_{0.5}$Al$_{1.0}$Fe$_{1.5}$O$_4$ (LAFO) thin films to be a promising model system for probing these mechanisms because of its distinct temperature dependent spin diffusion length (SDL) trends for electrically and thermally generated magnons. With increasing temperature, the electrical SDL shows minimal change, while the thermal SDL decreases. We attribute these trends to distinct magnon populations and scattering mechanisms: thermally generated high $k$ magnons are limited by magnon-phonon scattering, whereas electrically generated low $k$ magnons are limited by relaxational scattering from magnetic impurities.
summary: This paper investigates why different methods of generating magnons in LAFO thin films lead to opposite temperature dependencies in their spin diffusion lengths. By distinguishing between high-k (thermal) and low-k (electrical) magnon populations, the authors show that thermal magnons are limited by increasing phonon scattering, whereas electrical magnons are limited by impurity-driven scattering that decreases with temperature. This work provides a way to probe fundamental damping mechanisms in low-loss magnetic insulators, which are critical for future spintronic technologies.
main_problem: The study aims to understand the distinct damping mechanisms and temperature-dependent trends of spin diffusion lengths (SDL) for electrically versus thermally generated magnons in low-damping LAFO thin films.
main_result: The researchers found that thermal SDL decreases with temperature due to magnon-phonon scattering, while electrical SDL increases with temperature because the scattering from magnetic impurities (modeled as two-level systems) weakens as they approach saturation.
method: The study uses nonlocal transport measurements in Pt/LAFO devices, employing the Spin Hall Effect for electrical injection and the Spin Seebeck Effect for thermal generation, combined with mid-wave infrared microscopy for thermal mapping.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of Li0.5Al1.0Fe1.5O4 (LAFO) spinel ferrite thin films grown on MgAl2O4 substrates. The transport is modeled using a 1D magnon diffusion equation considering both magnon-number-conserving and non-conserving scattering channels.
key_observables: Spin diffusion length (SDL), nonlocal resistance amplitudes, Gilbert damping, and thermal decay length.
important_parameters: Temperature range (10 K to 280 K), film thicknesses (16 nm and 86 nm), and magnon energy cutoff (5.3 K).
main_assumptions: The model assumes isotropic magnon dispersion, a 1D diffusion approximation, and that compressive strain from the substrate is the primary driver of easy-plane anisotropy.
figures_summary: The notes do not provide specific figure descriptions, but imply figures showing temperature-dependent SDL, thickness-dependent transport, and structural characterization via XRD.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of magnon damping, describes the material growth and device fabrication, presents experimental transport and thermal imaging results, develops a theoretical diffusion model to explain the divergent SDL trends, and concludes with a comparison of scattering mechanisms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Nonlocal transport experimental setup. (a) Schematic of a device consisting of Pt injector and detector strips (width w) on a LAFO thin film grown on MgAl2O4 (MAO). Magnons are injected into LAFO electrically via the spin Hall effect (SHE) and thermally via the spin Seebeck ef- fect, and detected a distance d away via the inverse SHE. The LAFO magnetization M is rotated in-plane by an angle γ us- ing an in-plane external magnetic field H. The image above the schematic is a mid-wave infrared (MWIR) microscopy im- age taken at T = 300 K for a device with d = 5.0 µm when a 0.5 mA ac current is applied. (b, c) Nonlocal resistance (de- fined from harmonic voltages) as a function of...

FIG. 2. Nonlocal resistance amplitude as a function of injector-detector distance d for (a) electrically and (b) ther- mally generated magnons for select temperatures. Solid lines are fits to Eq. (3) and are used to extract λe,th m , and dashed lines are extrapolations beyond the fitted range. The data are well described by a single exponential decay over the selected distance range.

FIG. 3. Spin diffusion length as a function of temperature for thermally (λth m) and electrically generated magnons (λe m). Circles denote the experimental data extracted from fits to the nonlocal resistance using Eq. (3), while solid lines denote the theoretical modeling. The parameters for the model are listed in Supplemental Section VI [33].
